speaker: interviewee
text: The Palestine--

speaker: interviewer
text: Markheim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll two with Maurice Markheim. Roll two, Maurice Markheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had come back from this first camp, and this is where you found out that the Germans were really harsh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brutal, hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brutal.

speaker: interviewee
text: People were dying there. People had all-- all-- all kinds of sickness. They had a hospital there. They called it a hospital. On a bare concrete floor, people were laying there, waiting for the bed, already dead. And this, I'm talking about December 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back to Cracow, did people believe you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll-- I'll be honest with you. My mom, my family did believe me because I came out-- I came back like a musselmann. You know what's a musselmann? It's a guy that's just bone. And-- and-- and-- I was-- I lost so much weight and everything. The way I was neglected and the way I was looking, that I was-- I was afraid of my own shadow. So they let me go. They didn't bother me too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had they beaten you, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I got beaten, and how. Oh, I got beaten. For what? Well, maybe because I stopped, for a second, working.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Cracow before you got caught again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I was in Cracow-- it was quite a bit, because then later, they start to talk about the ghetto-- the Germans-- to form a ghetto. So what actually happens is they said, they're going to issue only so many-- so many IDs. These people-- they were changing all the time. These people were going to get the IDs. They will be permitted to live in the ghettos. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The ones who don't want, they don't care. Or come to-- or not disappear, in other words. You know what I mean. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the way the tragedies-- the tragedies-- here, where the tragedies start. They break up the family. OK? Me, my oldest brother, and my sister with a child-- she was married. She had a child from before. We didn't get the ID. Her husband was on the other side, in Lubawa. OK? My sister was there, too, but she came back. And then later on, what happened-- I have the pictures. And later on, what actually happened, from worrying about his wife, he-- my brother-in-law-- and their child, he committed suicide in Limburg. In Lubawa. So we were split. So my mother had some people known in a little town, maybe about 20 kilometers from Cracow, named Niepolomice. So my brother went there with my mom, and they rented an apartment for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, there's still Jews there. They don't bother them. It was like a small town. Very small town, maybe 2,000 families. Jewish families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she rented an apartment there. And we, for a while, we lived there-- my brother, my sister, my nephew, and me. We lived in that town. From what do we live? I'll be honest with you. Any job came along where I could make a few dollars to bring home-- because my sister was the oldest one. So I brought it home. So I got any job there. Well, I was working feeding-- feeding chickens and-- and goose. So I was stealing eggs. I was bringing her eggs. You know, you manipulate a little bit. But it wasn't bad. It wasn't bad. And whatever we could sell from our belongings, we were selling that, and we lived on that. Of course, your father was not working anymore, was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father was working in the-- no, my father wasn't with us. He was in the ghetto. He went to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had to support themselves because my father, my mother, my other sister-- my younger sister-- and my other brother, you see, he-- he-- he-- they remained-- they got the ID. My other brother, who escaped later from Plaszow, he went-- he went to Bochnia with his wife. He was married already. So he went with her family. You see, that's the way he split. And her family didn't get the IDs at all to live in the ghetto. You know, my--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was it like in this small town? Did you have a lot of difficulty with non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, I didn't. I'll be honest with you. Because maybe I didn't have such Jewish features, you know what I mean? Maybe I could get by. With my-- with my-- with my language, I didn't speak with an accent or anything. So maybe-- they knew. I'm sure they knew who I was. But I had no-- no-- I never had no problem. I'll be honest with you, no problem at all. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll-- I'll tell you in a minute. We got the best proof. Where we used to live, it cost us [SPEAKING POLISH]. That neighborhood was 90-- 90% gentile, maybe 10% Jews. I had no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were going to school with me. How could I have problems with them? We played soccer together. How would I have problems with them? If I wanted to go in another neighborhood, when I went with them, I was protected by them, if they want to beat up the Jews, you see? So everybody had a-- you know, like the Polacks used to say, also. Oh, this is my Jew. He's different. He's the only one Jew. He's different. This is my Jew. You know, that's the way they used to say. It-- it was true. So, there was-- there's no question about it, there was antisemitism there. I didn't feel it like that. Maybe I was too young to understand it, even. I wasn't interested in that. Now, what's the question? Now, it's fine. Then how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the little town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Niepolomice? Till-- till they make Niepolomice a judenrein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. They said, everybody from these vicinities around Niepolomice has to, after this certain date, find themselves in Wieliczka. Wieliczka, this is a little town 12 kilometers from Cracow. The famous salt mine was there. Maybe you've heard about there. And here, after that day, anybody who finds himself outside the line where they say is going to be shot. And that's the way they did it. One day, they round up all Wieliczka, and the deportations start. And here, the first time, what you-- you really saw what the Germans are. They were tearing at pieces children, babies-- throwing out through the windows, babies. Winding them up-- grab them by the leg, and turn them around and through the window, out, and shot them in the air. Old people-- two Germans, one-- one-- one picked up by leg, the other one by-- no. By the neck or by the hand, and throw them, you know, on the truck. Little-- they were still-- everybody was still alive. Like a sack of potatoes on the truck, on the truck, one on the top of the other. It was-- I don't know. I stood up there, and I said, my God. My God. It was a beautiful, hot day. Is there a God, is what they say. He is up there, and he looks on those things, what they do to us. No, there's no God. If he could be up there and just look at it and not to do anything-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people were dying from thirst. They used to go out way before three days. And in the meantime, they were going all over the town. People were hiding. They'd dig them out from the bunkers, shot them. So many dead people. It's unbelievable. And the rest of them, they took in the [INAUDIBLE], and the closest [INAUDIBLE], they snuck by the [INAUDIBLE] there, you see, from Wieliczka. And they shot the other people. There was-- there-- there-- there-- there-- there-- there-- there-- there-- The-- the gas chamber, like Auschwitz-- there was already Auschwitz, but it wasn't developed yet, you know, that you can absorb so many people, you see, or send any. They had to notify them before. This is what I found out later, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were shooting. Then they found out that shooting was too expensive so-- too expensive, because the bullet is about 20 fenings. Whatever it was, it's too expensive to kill the Jews. A Jew. So they developed-- they start to develop the gas, you see. They put more to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wieliczka, they picked up-- in Wieliczika-- oh, so what we did meantime, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we knew somebody-- not we. If I said, what I knew, I knew nothing. My sister, my-- my brother knew, and my father knew some people that had Argentina [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] citizenship. They're Argentinians. They're Jews, but Argentinian. So my brother went and took the baby-- my-- my nephew-- and my sister, shipped them way in ahead, smuggled them in, back to Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Cracow, they-- she got those papers, those false papers. She-- they lived outside the ghetto, like-- like-- like nobody would bother them. They were allowed. They were allowed. And we were-- me only, and my brother, in Wieliczka. You understand what I'm saying? Yes, but I'm waiting for Argentina.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Wieliczka.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Argentina?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We'll come to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll come to it. She was living for a long time outside the ghetto. Eventually, they picked her up, too, and sent her to Bergen-Belsen, and that was the end of it. I never heard from her anymore. I know only stories, what they were doing with that. They said-- a neighbor of ours-- once she told me, she was-- all the time, she was seeing my sister, she was seeing my-- my nephew. And that one day, they packed them all in the car. And German-- and they're not in the car. In the-- no. The railroads. They took them to the railroad station and the box cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a-- that German flyer, airplane, flew over, and they bombed everybody, the whole track, the whole-- the whole thing. So that's just a name, you know, that they didn't kill them. But listen, there's a war. Got-- got killed. You see? That's what my neighbor told us, when she saw her the last time. You see? That was with Argentina.

speaker: interviewee
text: But me and my brother, we remained in the deportation. So we were lucky. They picked 1,000 Jews-- young ones. You know, I was tall. I was-- I was healthy. I was young. To clean up the city from the dead bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Wieliczka. To clean that up. And we were all working so hard. While I was working, I was frightened. They sent me out to the cemetery to bury the-- the dead people, which they were bringing from the city, which they shot during the deportation. They had to clean it up. They can't leave that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my brother said, you know what? I'm going to go. You go at night, and I'll go during the day to work. So I went at night, and he went during the day.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to the barracks, they put us in one barrack. I remember the barrack on the main-- on the main highway, 1,000 people in one barrack. And one fine day, I'm coming back from work-- from-- from-- from the cemetery. And I saw my brother briefly. He was walking out, saying hi, hi. [COUGHS] Excuse me. Maybe about an hour later, a truck comes along, pulls up-- a truck with a few SS men. They need 100 volunteers. And I was by the doorway. And, you know, I was-- I had such a sad-- sad-- you know, face, and I was hungry. They were not giving me any food or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said-- I asked-- I asked the German-- he was such an innocent young fellow, the SS. He said, what for do they need the volunteer? He said, come. Come, come. It's good. It's good. It's a good-- it's a good place to go to work. And this was-- they picked-- they-- they need 100 people to Kabelwerker in Plaszow. That means cable works?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the first work. The Kabelwerker-- no, Kabelwerker-- this is-- 'werker' means factory. 'Kabel' is a cable, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's a cable factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Factory. And this is the first-- the beginning of cable factory, the first people would work before-- I mean, in the cable factory. Prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the cable used for? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- all kinds of cable for-- they used to tell us that not only cables we were doing, but we were doing-- doing things-- parts for submarines. You see? And cables for-- for communication, they were big cables, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that place called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kabelwerker. That's the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also--

speaker: interviewee
text: Double-- this was Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: The formal name was Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Plaszow. So they brought us to Julag. They didn't bring us to Kabelwerker. They brought us to Arbeitslager, Julag. Also Plaszow, but maybe about three or four kilometers away. And we're supposed to live in Julag and get all our food [INAUDIBLE] in Julag. And in the morning, they'd pick us up, walk, work the 12 hours, then came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's just happened that there was a bunch of people that the-- that the-- the director of Kabel-- that's a big factory. You know, over 2,000 people. I considered that was a big factory. This was before the war, but 2,000 people used to work there. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the way it was supervised by the Germans was a man named-- man named-- what  was his name? Baymer. I forgot his first name. I will-- I will refresh my memory.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was a man. A-- a-- you could see a-- a-- a better-- he was a Nazi, no question about it. Intelligent, a high integrity, and a high-class-- you could see it, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a man with influence because he took over. He came in, and he took over that factory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But he wasn't a womanizer. He was not doing any black market. And you couldn't even get thread and a needle. There were two fellows who were working before the war in the factory as a bookkeeper. Do you know that he found-- he took the-- and he found them-- they found themselves-- it's coincidence-- in our group. And he put them back in the office to be the bookkeepers. That's unusual, a Jew to have in a position like that, you know, in a German factory. But he was a gentleman. He never beat anybody. He never-- he never-- well, he was a Nazi. There's no question about it. He never sent anybody to dead, to die or anything. He never had anybody shot. He was a very, very decent man, and I'm surprised that it's true. The bottom line is he didn't want to get involved. And he wouldn't-- he wouldn't dare to get involved to save the Jews by the end of it like Schindler did, but he was taking care of them-- of us. And we were there maybe for two years. We became the property of the SS-- SS later, because they changed the Arbeitslager to concentration camp. But, you see, I got myself ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were going to the factory, to the Julag, to this camp here. We-- the-- the condition was horrible there. Lice, you name it-- lice. You know what's lice? Epidemic. Hunger, killing-- you have no idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: The commandant-- the commandant of that camp was named Miller, a young fellow, maybe early thirties. He was a monster. But you know, you got used to him. You never-- you never pray for a new king. You took the old one you were used to already. You don't want a new one.

speaker: interviewer
text: We told that to Baymer. And Baymer went to the gestapo. And he said, whatever he doing, he said, you know what? They are professional, and I train them even more. I want protect those Jews or some of these Jews. So they want-- the-- the gestapo asked him, what-- what-- what-- what do you want from us? He said, those conditions where they live, it's impossible for them to work during-- to work during the day. Without any question. Now, at that time, it was still the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: It took us-- they took us to the ghetto there, the SS. And they went to the Judenrat. And they said, look, here's 100 people. You have to locate them. You have to put them in the ghetto. You have to give them room to live. Food, they're going to get it on the job. And that's the way it was. Well, as time goes by, they were cleaning up ghetto, they were making the ghetto smaller, deportation. Smaller the ghetto. They make a Ghetto A-- a Ghetto A, a Ghetto B. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Meantime, they were building, on the main Plaszow, barracks already. They made Plaszow-- if I said, this was located right on the spot where, before the war, there was a new Jewish cemetery. On that cemetery, they built the-- the barrack. And believe you me, the first barrack that was finished, they took the hundred people from ghetto and sent them up there. There was-- there was a guard still under supervised-- supervision, excuse me-- from Miller, from downstairs. Because, you see, Julag was downstairs, down-- down below on the main highway. And this was up in the hill a little bit. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we were working and going from over there to Kabel. To-- to Kabelwerker. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All of a sudden, they built, they built, they put more people from the ghetto up to Plaszow there. And we were bringing them. It was making smaller. Some of them, they went to Auschwitz, some of them, they were here. Oh, I forgot. My brother, let him rest in peace. I went to Kabelwerker. And my-- and-- and-- and my brother went to Stalowa Wola, another camp someplace else, more east. So we got separated, you see. And I lost track of him. And later, they said that they brought-- they brought-- they [INAUDIBLE] camp, and they brought him over here. But anyway, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as they build the barrack, and that they built the fence around the camp, on the top there. On the top. And-- and they developed what they called that Hujowa Gorka, the [INAUDIBLE]. And they were making different projects. You know, different trades, they were working. They had all the trades there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One fine day, a new-- a new commandant came. They liquidate Julag down there. Miller was out, they sent him on the front. And the new commandant came. And here came the monster, Otto Goth-- I think Otto Goth. My God, when I saw him the first time-- just for looking at him [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was unbearable. It's unbelievable, what a brutal man. He just ate an apple here and then pow, pow, pow-- shot people like-- like nothing, for no reason. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The same time, Hujowa Gorka was developed. You known, Hujowa Gorka is that place where it's mass graves, mass graves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Over there, they brought the body. They dump them. [SPEAKING POLISH]-- it means like a Caterpillar. You know, a big truck would-- would pick up the dirt and just throw it on there, that's it. The way you're found. They were clothed, not clothed. That's the way they were dead, laying there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The next-- the next people from there, they liquidate the ghetto. From all the ghetto, they shoot. Most of the times, they killed the people. You see? Before the deportation-- excuse me, when I was in the Julag, there was a deportation in the ghetto, too. My father was shot in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, at that time, at the hospital. And the whole hospital-- with the doctors and the nurses, with everybody, they rounded up the hospital, and they-- they took them to [NON-ENGLISH]. And now, [NON-ENGLISH] is a big-- is a big no. Marketplace. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Over there, they were-- they were standing with a machine gun, and they were killing them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then my father was shot. And my mother and my sister, they were sent to Belzec, you see. They killed them in Belzec. I didn't know, from those days, because I was up there. If I knew, so what will happen? It will be more tragic for me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The condition of Plaszow, by God, was unbelievable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How unbelievable that you can see-- if you see-- if you see the movie you know, it's too hard to describe. You know, you can't-- if you live a story, you can't put on a paper everything. You can't write on a paper everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maurice, what in particular was not in the movie that you wish had been in the movie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe while I'm talking to you, maybe I will-- maybe we'll-- my computer will set it back. You see? There is-- there is-- there is no-- you can't. It's almost unbelievable. And I made a statement like that. You know, you write a story. You can't-- the paper-- you can't write everything on your paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mention one thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of things. But I tell you the truth, if I were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [GROANS] My personal friend-- I showed a picture from him with you. I had to watch him, the way they hanged him. The way they hanged them, two of them. From one, I have, still, a picture from before the war, which I will show you later. And the other one, I didn't have a picture. And we had to look at it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My very good friend Denik Fuchs. His name was Denik Fuchs. [DRINKS] I don't know from where to start first. To tell-- to give you an example. Maybe I-- maybe I can think of it so I can put together one-- one sentence so I'll be able to-- to-- to show you, to prove it to you what kind of man he could be. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, just the word, Goth is coming or he will go by-- you're-- you're-- the death went over you. You feel-- you felt already dead. You just-- you were just waiting because you didn't know from where he was going to come. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I'm trying to say, you can't-- you can't put everything on the paper, you want to write a story, right? And from the story, if you want to make a picture and put them on the screen, impossible to put everything on the screen that occurred. But I've got to give credit to Spielberg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] With using a little bit of intelligence, he put the best he could.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I'm sure, if he will put a little bit more, people wouldn't go and see it. That kind of [INAUDIBLE] will be too much, they-- especially the young people, they will not see it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You see, because he could put more. He could put much more, speaking about Goth only. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Stehbunker. You know a stehbunker? That little box-- I don't know what it's about, about-- about two feet by two feet. You were standing 72 hours, you couldn't move till you were lucky. OK? My brother was once there. He came out after 72 hours, that box. It was sunk-- sunk in the ground?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, standing!

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a box. You would stand, or you would lie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stand. Oh, no. Oh lie is OK. Stand. You couldn't sit down. You couldn't kneel. But you know, for you to sit down only, you couldn't. He came out from it one day, he had legs like this, swollen. But he was lucky. He was lucky that he didn't kill him. The same time, killing was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he have to go into that box?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why he had to-- he was once caught. He was an outside commander going to work outside the-- the Plaszow. And-- and-- you know, a Pole handed him a bread, and he smuggled that in to their camp because I had connections with the Poles, with the-- you know, with the outside because I was working with them. Like, for example, I was working with a man. He likes me. He was a Polish guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was going home. We were going to the camp. OK? I used to tell him, look, go in this and this alleyways to this is and this people. Tell him I'm here, and I'm hungry. They will pay you for it, and you're going bring me some food. They done it. You see? But he risks, too, because if they will catch him, they kill him. He risks his life, too. But not-- that just was very little chance to be caught, you see. And this stays. Later, this stops, too, because we were still having-- we-- being there, let's put it this way. We were hungry. OK? You couldn't live, and you couldn't die. You were-- they were holding us like just between, trying to make-- little by little-- zombie out of me. Out of us. So you know, if you got with your stomach-- if your stomach is hungry, when you're thinking only about that, you don't think about something else. And eventually, even if you're not hungry, you believe you're hungry. It's all human mind. It's all in your mind. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK? So I was working-- we were working there. But we knew everything, what's going on up there. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] We saw him a few times. He come to visit people and, you know, us and the Kabelwerker. Oh, oh, wait a minute. I got lost. Goth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got lost, honey. Just a moment, just a moment. How was it-- wait a minute, yeah. So we were going from the camp up there, from Goth, main camp, to the Kabelwerker. We went, and we complained to the-- what's going on, the conditions that it was. At that time, there were trying-- they were-- not trying. They were liquidating the ghetto, killing young-- young people who work on a good-- for a good company up-- up to the-- to the-- to the main camp. And he went, and he took care-- Baymer, The director of the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: The good guy?

speaker: interviewee
text: The good guy, yeah. And he said to the [NON-ENGLISH], I remember that was the headquarter from the gestapo. And he had permission to build few barracks [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the property of the Kabelwerker. And they built barracks on Kabelwerker for 300 people. Half were woman, and half were men.
